questions.question_text: Florida law requires how many subscribing witnesses for a deed conveying real property to be valid?
questions.explanation: Section 689.01, F.S., requires the grantor to sign a deed in the presence of two subscribing witnesses for the deed to validly convey real property — witnesses go to the deed's validity, not merely recording. Acknowledgment (notarization) is the separate requirement for recording under s. 695.03. Since 2020, witnessing may also be completed remotely through Florida's online notarization procedures.

questions.question_text: Which type of deed provides the greatest protection to the buyer in a Florida transaction?
questions.explanation: A general warranty deed provides the greatest protection because the grantor warrants title against all defects, including those that occurred before the grantor's ownership. A special warranty deed only warrants against defects during the grantor's ownership period.

questions.question_text: An executor of a deceased person's estate conveys property to a beneficiary using which type of deed?
questions.explanation: A personal representative's deed is used by an executor or administrator of an estate to transfer property. A guardian's deed is used by a court-appointed guardian, which is a different situation.

questions.question_text: A grantor wants to transfer whatever interest they may have in a property without any warranties. Which deed should be used?
questions.explanation: A quitclaim deed conveys whatever interest the grantor has (if any) with no warranties whatsoever. It is commonly used between family members, to clear title defects, or to transfer interest between spouses.

questions.question_text: For a Florida deed to be recorded, it must be acknowledged by means of:
questions.explanation: Acknowledgment (notarization) is required for recording in Florida. Along with two witnesses and the grantor's signature, notarization confirms the grantor's identity and voluntary execution of the deed.

questions.question_text: Florida uses which type of recording statute to determine priority between competing claims to real property?
questions.explanation: Florida's recording act (s. 695.01, F.S.) is a notice statute: an unrecorded conveyance is void against a subsequent bona fide purchaser for value without notice, regardless of who records first. Prompt recording still matters — it gives constructive notice, which prevents anyone who buys later from qualifying as a purchaser without notice.

questions.question_text: Where are deeds recorded in Florida?
questions.correct_answer: Clerk of the Circuit Court in the property's county
questions.explanation: Florida deeds are recorded with the Clerk of the Circuit Court in the county where the property is located. Recording provides constructive notice — legal notice to the world of the ownership interest.

questions.question_text: The Florida Marketable Record Title Act establishes a root of title period of how many years?
questions.explanation: The Marketable Record Title Act (Chapter 712, F.S.) simplifies title searches by establishing a 30-year root of title. Claims or interests not re-recorded within the 30-year period may be extinguished.

questions.question_text: Recording a deed in Florida's public records provides what type of notice to the world?
questions.explanation: Recording provides constructive notice — the legal presumption that everyone has been notified of the ownership interest, whether or not they actually checked the records. This protects the recorded owner against later claims.

questions.question_text: A buyer purchases a home but does not record the deed. A subsequent buyer who records first and has no knowledge of the prior sale will:
questions.correct_answer: Have priority as a bona fide purchaser without notice
distractors: Lose to the first buyer regardless
Need FREC approval to claim title
questions.explanation: Under Florida's notice statute (s. 695.01, F.S.), an unrecorded deed is void against a subsequent purchaser for value without notice. The second buyer prevails because they purchased without notice of the prior sale — not because they recorded first. Prompt recording is still critical: it gives constructive notice that cuts off any later purchaser's bona fide status.
